title: Remarks at the APEC Finance Ministers Meeting

paragraphs.text: Good morning, and welcome to San Francisco. As Chair of this year's APEC Finance Ministers process, I am honored to host my counterparts from across the Asia-Pacific. This region represents roughly sixty percent of global GDP and nearly half of all international trade. The decisions we make here reverberate across the world economy, and I believe this year's gathering comes at a particularly consequential moment.

The global economy has demonstrated remarkable resilience over the past year. Growth has held up better than many predicted, labor markets have remained strong in much of the APEC region, and inflation — while still above target in several economies — has come down substantially from its peak. The United States has navigated the post-pandemic adjustment better than almost anyone expected, with growth accelerating even as inflation has fallen.

But I want to be candid about the risks that lie ahead. Geopolitical tensions, including the conflict in the Middle East, the ongoing war in Ukraine, and broader strategic competition, threaten to fragment the global economy in ways that would be costly for all of us. Supply chain disruptions, commodity price volatility, and heightened uncertainty can undermine the growth prospects of every economy in this room.

The United States has been clear that we do not seek to decouple from any economy. What we seek is de-risking — diversifying our supply chains to reduce dangerous concentrations, protecting our national security in narrow and well-defined areas, and ensuring that the economic relationship between major powers rests on a foundation of fairness and reciprocity. These objectives are consistent with, not opposed to, a robust multilateral trading system.

The Indo-Pacific Economic Framework for Prosperity, which the United States launched with twelve partner economies, is designed to strengthen economic ties in this region on the basis of high standards and shared interests. We have made substantial progress on supply chain resilience, clean energy, anti-corruption, and tax policy. IPEF is not a traditional trade agreement, but it addresses twenty-first century economic challenges that traditional agreements have often overlooked.

Supply chain resilience is perhaps the most urgent of these challenges. The pandemic exposed how a single disruption in one part of the world can cascade through interconnected production networks, causing shortages of everything from semiconductors to medical supplies. We learned that efficiency and resilience are not the same thing, and that just-in-time supply chains, while cost-effective in normal times, can be catastrophically fragile when shocks occur.

Through IPEF, we are building an early warning system for supply chain disruptions, establishing protocols for crisis coordination among partner economies, and identifying critical sectors where diversification of sourcing and production is most needed. This is not about excluding any country from global trade — it is about building redundancy so that no single point of failure can bring entire industries to a halt.

Debt sustainability in the Asia-Pacific and beyond remains a serious concern. Many developing economies entered the pandemic with already elevated debt levels, and the combination of fiscal stimulus, currency depreciation, and rising interest rates has pushed debt burdens to unsustainable levels in some countries. Sri Lanka's crisis was a warning, and the restructuring processes in Ghana, Zambia, and Ethiopia have shown how complex and prolonged sovereign debt resolution can be.

The United States has been a strong advocate for reforms to the international debt architecture. We have pushed for greater transparency in lending terms, more timely and comprehensive debt restructuring through the Common Framework, and fair burden-sharing among all creditors — including non-traditional bilateral lenders and private sector creditors. The principle is simple: when a country's debt is unsustainable, all creditors must contribute to the solution.

Climate finance is inextricably linked to development finance, and the Asia-Pacific region sits at the nexus of both challenges. Many APEC economies are among the most vulnerable to climate change — rising sea levels, extreme weather events, agricultural disruption — while also being home to some of the world's fastest-growing sources of emissions. Addressing this dual reality requires massive investment in clean energy, adaptation infrastructure, and natural capital.

The United States has made the largest climate investment in its history through the Inflation Reduction Act, committing hundreds of billions of dollars to clean energy deployment, manufacturing, and innovation. This is not only an environmental policy — it is an economic strategy that is already creating jobs and attracting private investment. We believe that other APEC economies can benefit from adopting similarly ambitious approaches tailored to their own circumstances.

Mobilizing private capital for climate investment in developing economies is essential. Public finance alone cannot close the gap. The Treasury Department has been working with multilateral development banks to reform their approach — using concessional capital more strategically to de-risk private investment, developing new financial instruments like green bonds and sustainability-linked loans, and creating project preparation facilities that bring bankable deals to market.

Let me also address the importance of a strong and well-governed international financial architecture. The International Monetary Fund and the World Bank must be adequately resourced and effectively governed to meet twenty-first century challenges. The United States supports quota reforms at the IMF that reflect the evolution of the global economy, and we have championed the evolution agenda at the World Bank to better address global challenges alongside its core development mission.

Financial inclusion in the Asia-Pacific region has made extraordinary progress, driven in large part by digital financial services. But significant gaps remain, particularly for women, rural populations, and micro and small enterprises. As finance ministers, we have a responsibility to ensure that the benefits of financial innovation reach those who need them most, while also managing the risks that new technologies can introduce.

On digital assets and the regulation of the crypto ecosystem, the events of the past year — including the collapse of FTX and the failures of several major crypto lenders — have underscored the need for robust regulation. The United States is working to develop a comprehensive regulatory framework that protects consumers and investors, safeguards financial stability, and prevents the use of digital assets for illicit finance, while still allowing responsible innovation.

Tax policy cooperation among APEC economies is another area where progress has been significant. The global agreement on a minimum corporate tax rate of fifteen percent represents a historic achievement in international tax cooperation. Implementation is now underway across multiple jurisdictions, and I am encouraged by the broad support this initiative has received. A fair international tax system prevents a race to the bottom and ensures that multinational corporations pay their fair share wherever they operate.

As I look around this table, I see an extraordinary concentration of economic dynamism and potential. The Asia-Pacific region has lifted more people out of poverty in the past fifty years than any region in human history. Trade, investment, innovation, and human capital development have driven this transformation. Our challenge now is to sustain this momentum while addressing the new complexities of geopolitical competition, climate change, and technological disruption.

The United States remains deeply committed to the Asia-Pacific region — as a trading partner, an investor, a security partner, and a collaborator on the great challenges of our time. We do not ask any economy to choose between partners. We ask only that the rules of the road are fair, transparent, and consistently applied. That is what APEC was built to achieve, and it is what we must continue to pursue together.

In closing, I want to thank the APEC Secretariat and the many officials who have worked tirelessly to prepare for this meeting. The Joint Ministerial Statement we will adopt today reflects months of careful negotiation and genuine compromise. It is a testament to the enduring value of multilateral economic cooperation. Let us carry this spirit forward as we navigate the challenges and opportunities ahead. Thank you.
